posts.name: Chris Barton
posts.cooked: For all it’s top wines there are a fair amount of Vosne or Vosne adjacent (NSG) wines that aren’t completely astronomical in price. I’m curious what bottles people think are good QPR in Vosne that are more or less below $300. Not necessarily the “best” wine but those that provide a lot for the cost. I was thinking of bottles like Berthaut-Gerbet VR village, Mongeard Mugneret 1ers, Clavelier 1ers, L’Arlot ‘Suchots’, Jean Marc Millot ‘Echezeaux’…

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked: Aurelien comes to mind

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: hudelot suchots, millot suchots, jerome chezeaux suchots

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Domaine Coquard Loison-Fleurot

Wines are supposedly made by Sebastián Cathiard’s bestie.

posts.cooked: Meo NSG Boudots. Always a ringer for Vosne.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked: Bouchard and Jadot Vosne Suchots

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne, Clavelier Liex-dits and brulees, Beamonts, but my favourite is Dujac Malconsorts, but probably now above threshold.

posts.name: Chris. Mc Cart
posts.cooked:
QPR?
You mention Berthaut-Gerbert village, but you can get (for now) both her Petit Monts & Suchots for less. Michel Gros is still very well priced given holdings and track record. Confuron-Cotetidot Suchots? Clavalier village La Combe Brulee is under $100 and high quality and worth looking for in solar vintages.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I like the wines from Hudelot-Noellat. Mongeard Mugneret has traditionally been an excellent source of value for Echezeaux and Grands Echezeaux, but I find the wines a bit firm when young and IMHO they need time - although they do very well with time. I have not had much wine from Millot but what I have had has been excellent.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: L’Arlot Suchots is a great value. Some of these others mentioned are getting very hard to find.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
$300 is a high threshold. At that level, you get lots of phenomenal stuff. Most have been mentioned. Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne AOC, Hudelot Noellat Suchots, L’Arlot Suchots, and all the Clavelier Vosne 1ers come to mind.

The more interesting question (to me) is what you can find for ~$100 in Vosne (now that the wines listed above have all comfortably exceeded that threshold). At that level, while I think it’s only been made since relatively recently, Drouhin’s Vosne 1er Cru is very nice (as its Chambolle 1er Cru is for Chambolle). And in one that never is discussed, Remoriquet makes a servicable (certainly tasty) Malconsorts for less than $100. Cathiard it’s not, but it’s real Vosne nonetheless.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked:
I’ve wanted to try Gerard Mugneret, whose Suchots and Boudots are still priced fairly. Just a year or two ago the Brulees was priced similarly but all the offers I see currently wouldn’t qualify as ‘value’. It has been suggested that they are reasonable still because the labels are so ugly . I even tried ordering a mixed case a few years back from Morrell as prearrivals but they failed to deliver.

As Yao was suggesting I bought Forey Echezeaux for this for a few years starting in 2010, am wondering when I need to try the first bottle. I expect no time soon; they are supposed to be rustic / old school. His Petits Monts I’ve tried have run quite a bit less, come from a fairly prestigious vineyard, and have been approachable and satisfying if not mind-blowingly great.

It’s not Vosne proper but since this thread took a detour down to Nuits St. Georges, FWIW I’ve been liking the vintages of Ch. de la Tour Clos Vougeot VV I’ve tried. If you shop smart it can be occasionally well under $200 and drinking it doesn’t make me think ‘value’ wine.

posts.name: Chris Barton
posts.cooked: Thanks for all the replies. My inquiry was meant to be broad and I wanted to see what people thought of as good value, not necessarily value wines. That’s also why I mentioned village wines and wines up to $300 because I wanted the option available to pick some GCs that might still be good values just as a great village wine can be a good value. Under $100 is fascinating but also narrows it quite a bit…

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked: Bit boring this. Same old names. A new one here: Robert Sirugue. He makes wines under his own name and for another Domaine (sorry, I forget the name.) You can pick up his Vosne AC for well under $100 if you look hard enough.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
James, I recall buying some of these from '99 to '01 which were excellent. Jolted my menory also some good wines from teh same era from Herve Sigaut. Another domaine we don’t hear much of.

This is another obvious one but I have had a number of good Faiveley Vosne Chaumes. Also Louis Jadot Vosne village and Beauxmonts can be quite decent, but need real patience.

Hudelot Noellat Vosne Beaumonts and Suchots can be top notch but I am uncertain of their pricing these days, but suspect they have crept up a fair bit.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey

Up this thread I forgot to mention my favorite Vosne QPR:

Bruno Clair’s Vosne ‘Champs Perdrix’ is a villages, but it’s from 70+ year old vines and the vineyard abuts La Tache. It’s consistently excellent.

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
Coincidentally, just received an offer for a suite of Michel Gros Vosne premier crus? Any experiences especially wrt to oak treatment?

They age fine and the oak integrates very well - I would give the 1ers at least 10-12 years.

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
Up this thread I forgot to mention my favorite Vosne QPR:

Bruno Clair’s Vosne ‘Champs Perdrix’ is a villages, but it’s from 70+ year old vines and the vineyard abuts La Tache. It’s consistently excellent.

Yes, on the map it´s across the road from La Tâche, but in reality it´s a drop of almost 10 meters, completely different terroir and micro climate -
and not really cheap.

posts.name: J0hn
posts.cooked:
Coincidentally, just received an offer for a suite of Michel Gros Vosne premier crus? Any experiences especially wrt to oak treatment?

My experience with Michel Gros is somewhat limited, so take this with a grain of salt. But the older vintages I’ve tried (not necessarily Vosne) have been big and oaky and unbalanced for my tastes. But the bottlings I’ve tried from the 2012 vintage forward (including some Vosne) have been much more balanced and interesting. I’ve been buying a bunch for the cellar this year from 2012 and newer.

I think all the confuron wines are good value. I tried some confuron gindre wines which were great.

C-Gindre wines need A LOT of time, very structured, even sometimes slightly dry stemmy tannins … even the Bg. rouge needs usually 10+ years. A VR Chaumes was fine with 24 years…
(all imho and subjectively)

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
im surprised not to see more mentions of Jadot in this thread. is that due to lack of value or people not liking the Vosnes?

I guess it’s what you’re after. I think post Lardiere the “wait 20 years” stuff is overstated, but they’re not great wines. To me, Jadot does their best work in Gevrey, not in Vosne. I’d rather drink an Arnoux-Lachaux Vosne village or a Mugneret-Gibourg Chaignots than a Jadot 1er cru Vosne is the way I’d put it.

posts.name: Gary Zimberg
posts.cooked:
Coincidentally, just received an offer for a suite of Michel Gros Vosne premier crus? Any experiences especially wrt to oak treatment?

My experience with Michel Gros is somewhat limited, so take this from But the older vintages I’ve tried (not necessarily Vosne) have been big and oaky and unbalanced for my tastes. But the bottlings I’ve tried from the 2012 vintage forward (including some Vosne) have been much more balanced and interesting. I’ve been buying a bunch for the cellar this year from 2012 and newer.

I had a very nice visit there last month and tasted mostly 18’s and 19’s (but also the '17 Clos Vougeot), and did not find any excess oak sticking out - but I might be more tolerant than some.
All of the '19 Village (VR, Chambolle and Nuit) were great for village level wines. The Clos des Reas and Brulees were the standouts -
I was very pleasantly surprised with their 18 & 19 Hautes Cotes and find it a great QPR and much better than the 2010 and 2012 which were the only other two vintages I have had (and still have).
Happy to be adding these to my cellar

_DSC4771.JPG4162×4840 2.26 MB

disclaimer, I am his US importer.

I would have said Guyon wines 6 to 12 months ago as great Vosne wines on an absolute and value/QPR basis, but after seeing the 2020 prices, I’m afraid these have become fairly priced but great wines

my vote goes to CLF, <$60 for his Vosne village and <$200 for his Echezeaux. Very reasonable when compared to market prices for other producers where village/1er cru/grand crus are easily 50-100% higher, but are probably same ballpark in terms of quality

posts.name: Gary Zimberg

I did not try the Aux Vallon (but bought a few of the '19), but I did taste the Fontaine St Martin and bought 6 each of the '18 and '19! I only took 1 of the 2019 HCdN to round up on a case.

Thanks. I haven’t tried any of the 2019 yet, but hope to soon.

I bought quite a few of the 2019 Vintage. Depending on how much you ordered, who you used to import to the US, and how you want to break down shipping costs - this was the pricing ex-domaine. Really hard to beat this for QPR in Vosne.

Appellation Unit Price (EUR) Price with Import (USD)
Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Nuits Au Vallon €12.50 [$27.41]
Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Nuits Rouge Fontaine Saint Martin - MONOPOLE €13.33 [$28.44]
Vosne Romanée €38.33 [$59.44]
Chambolle Musigny €38.33 [$59.44]
Nuits Saint Georges Les Chaliots €28.33 [$47.04]
Morey Saint Denis “En la Rue de Vergy” €29.17 [$48.08]
Gevrey-Chambertin “La Platière” €29.17 [$48.08]
Nuits Saint Georges 1er Cru €48.33 [$71.84]
Vosne Romanée 1er Cru Aux Brûlées €62.50 [$89.41]
Vosne Romanée 1er Cru Clos des Réas - MONOPOLE €65.83 [$93.54]
Clos Vougeot €98.33 [$133.84]


Sorry for the formatting - it doesn’t seem to want to take BB tables

I paid almost the exact same prices but my shipping was 11Euro/btl including local refrigerated delivery to NY (based on 5cs all together from my trip).
I am very happy I took a 6 pack of the 18 and 19 Fontaine St Martin. Many of the other wines I was buying the left overs where only 2 or 3 bottles were left.
There was no MSD or Gevrey available when I was there. Also was only able to buy single bottles of '17 CV (someone cancelled) and they were sold out on the '18 & '19

Gary

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
As Mr Zhu points out, the pricing ex cellars chez Guyon for the '20s is up some 30% for everything up to the Vosne wines, and then literally up 100% for everything Vosne villages and above.
Jean-Pierre knows how good the wines are, knows how much it has cost him in investments to get to such a level of quality, and is confident in the quality relative to his peers.
I can’t really argue, even if I find it disappointing.
I tried to sound the alarm bell as loud and clear as I could, for as long as I could, encouraging people to get what they can before the world and pricing catches on.
'Tis what it is.
I imagine that I will send out the offer later this winter for the '20s.

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,

On the highest level LA ROMEE was nominated as one of the truly greats of the 2020 vintage. William Kelley will probably not like it but NEAL MARTIN thought LA GRANDE RUE was a decent enough equivalent on that high level for those whose pockets were not that deep. I see that 2 of the best current critics have different opinions on one producer. Always possible of course but I regret that too many buyers base their acquisitions solely on critics. Who is right then? William or Neal? As an aside in my very humble opinion -I am certainly no match for William Kelley’s palate and I mean that- the wines by MONGEARD-MUGNERET nearly alwas lacked mid palate thickness and tasted a bit dried out after a ffew years in the bottle. I did not find that in LAMARCHE’s wines. But then again… Who am I??? Lots of respect for both William and Neal !
SINCERELY JOHAN

Johan…I am your friend and never wish to get into an argument with you.

I would like to just clarify one important point - which is William K review of the performance of Nicole Lamarche’s performance on 2015 red ( repeat on Doamine Lamarche 2015 reds). We are not talking about general performance …which should be an entirely difference subject.

Here is what I would like to talk to you about what you posted here which I have problem with :

***I investigated a little deeper: BETTANE & DESSAUVE rated the LAMARCHE very highly and awarded the domaine 4 stars. Meadows also rate their wines very highly ****

For your above text : are you talking that Bettane rated the Lamarchne very hightly in general term or about 2015 vintage ? I have no arguments if the comments are for general purpose.

Here is my bigger problem with your text : ** Meadows also rated their wine very highly **

Yes - Meadows did rated Lamarche wines very highly…but I investigated a little deeper and here is what I found he following text in Meadows Issue 65 ( 2015 CdeNuits red ) for Domaine Lamarhce :

(((((…I found the Lamarche 2015s to depart somewhat from the typical pattern I discovered elsewhere. This is to say that the real strength of their line-up is in the grands crus whereas often in 2015 it is the entry level wines that outperform. To be sure, the range of regional wines offers solid quality as well though I found the 1ers, Malconsorts and Suchots aside, to be of average quality in the context of the vintage…)))

Please let me know…what is your interpretation of the above text ?

My understanding is : Nicole did not perform well with vintage 2015 - espcially at the lower levels.

So Johan…may I say it is two against two ?

I do not own any wines from Lamarche 20915. Otherwise I will do a G-Ech 2015 horizontal by matching Lamarche with a few other producers.

If you do, please post …after the event.

SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Sorry that I am


Just for purpose of completeness - here is a copy of the text of what William Kelley said about Lamarche 2015 ( in this Broad in another thread : WK’s 020 and 019 Burgundy Report started on 2022/01/20) :


*******Nick Templeton wrote: ↑Fri Jan 21, 2022 2:16 am
Does anybody know why William stopped reviewing the Lamarche wines, after the 2016 vintage, or perhaps William may be able to respond? He described Nicole Lamarche as one of his favourite winemakers, at the time, so just seems a bit odd that her wines are no longer being reviewed. I would have liked to see his views for this vintage, although other reviews that I have seen, have been favourable.

Willam Kelly reply -

I certainly didn’t describe her as one of my favorite winemakers!

To be frank, despite talk of the wines getting better, I continue to think that they fall far short of the quality level to which they should aspire.

My comments at the time were:

Having heard much about alleged improvements at Domaine Lamarche, my visit here to taste the bottled 2015s left me disappointed. The domaine’s roster of appellations is formidable, and Nicole Lamarche declares that her aim is to capture elegance, freshness and fruit—an ambition that, in its own terms, I warmly applaud and would be delighted to champion. Elegance, however, is in the eye of the beholder, and I found these wines attenuated, diffuse and drying. ******

posts.cooked:
Another vote for Gerard Mugneret. His Vosne 1ers are reasonably priced, but the Boudots is the best QPR.

Unfortunately his wines will soon be out of this conversation, though. Prices have already started to creep up.

posts.name: Bertrand Leulliette
posts.cooked:
There are a few “under the radar” producers in Vosne because they are either too small or their winery is not in Vosne proper… but I feel like the likes of Robert Sirugue and Michel Noellat’s wines have really improved over the past 5 years. I read the original post with the mention of $300 as a ceilingand can’t help but suggest drining Echezeaux from producers that have Beaux Monts, Suchots or les ROuges in their range as there is a good chace these plots are fairly close to one another.

It is also important to note that there are changes with the Gros Estates as there is a division of some of the Gros Frere & Soeur vines that are split amongst Anne-Francoise, Michel and Anne and will see these Domaines add new cuvees.

I can name Domaine Desauney-Bissey but also Domaine Sirugue-Noellat (also called Arnaud & Sophie, from the union of the both Noellat and Sirugue children) as great an affordable wines with phenomenal plots.

FULL DISCLOSURE: I import Sirugue-Noellat, Anne Gros and Desauney-Bissey but most importantly spend roughly 3 months a year in the region that has been my passion for 20 years and now my own company since 2015.

I can name Domaine Desauney-Bissey but also Domaine Sirugue-Noellat (also called Arnaud & Sophie, from the union of the both Noellat and Sirugue children) as great an affordable wines with phenomenal plots.

Any more info or sources on what’s going on with the Gros Estates? I follow them pretty closely, so I’m interested what’s happening and why.

This is part of the Inheritance that was put in place by Colette Gros (Gros Frere & Soeur) in 1998, if you look at the family tree you can see that there were 3 branches that turned into 4 and the original plots are now split back into the the 3 original branches which means that each Estate will get more vines, in the case of Anne I believe it is Grands Echezeaux, Echezeaux Vignes Blanches, Vosne-Romanee Aux Communes and Bourgogne.

This is really well explained on the Michel Gros website but for some reason it looks like it is down right now.

Family trees are fascinating and Vosne has one of the most complex as literally everyone is somehow part of a very small handful of family trees.

Arbre-généalogique-2020-website-canva.jpeg-768x576.jpg768×576 57.4 KB

posts.name: Josh Najjar
posts.cooked:
Dear William,

I investigated a little deeper: BETTANE & DESSAUVE rated the LAMARCHE very highly and awarded the domaine 4 stars. Meadows also rate their wines very highly.Just goes to show not all critics share the same opinion. Who is right? Who is wrong? At the time Parker could destroy a domaine’s reputation. Such was his influence…Lots of customers decide on what to buy based on wine journals’ evaluations. I know a well known importer in Belgium who was asked at the time to solely look for 96-100 wines in Parker’s WA…
SINCERLY JOHAN

I had the '02 Lamarche - VR - Malconsorts last night. While it didn’t have any ‘Vosne spice’, it was a meaty concentrated wine with nice complexity, and a very enjoyable drink.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Had a 17 Domaine d’Eugenie VR Clos d’Eugenie last night. Modern, some oak, spice and slightly animale. Not QPR!

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
Forey is still my go to for value from Vosne.

The village is silly good, and the Bourgogne Rouge is out of this world.

This! Forey Vosne and Morey are just stunning values. 2019 VR was $AU99 = $US70 retail.

The 2019 Forey Vosne is a step behind his Petit Monts imo, but the 2019 is really QPR. Not rustic but refined and balanced with soft tannins and wonderful perfume. Wait 15-20 years
It is providing great pleasure for less than 50 Euro

20220207_182356.jpg4032×3024 1.63 MB

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,

Value in VOSNE? Go to R SIRUGUE and try to gety hold of his PETITS MONTS and/or GRANDS ECHEZEAUX. This domaine may not be your LEROY equivalent but I could not care less…

SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Ofer Refaeli
posts.cooked:
I highly recommend Domaine Daniel Rion:
-Les Beaux-Monts
-Les Chaumes
-Village
Great Vosne QPR.

posts.name: Daniel
posts.cooked:
@Bertrand_Leulliette Have you uncovered any more info on what the division of Colettes’s estate (Gros Frere et Soeur) will be?

I’ve found the following two snippets:

2021 will be last year here for Vosne monopole Clos de la Fontaine – it will be moving to Gros Frère & Soeurs. But the recompense for this domaine will be that the Clos de la Fontaine will be replaced with Clos de Vougeot from both the ‘Musigni’ and ‘Garenne’ sectors, plus some Echézeaux.

Burgundy-Report – 30 Jul 20

AF Gros – 2019

Tasted in Beaune with Matthias Parent and Caroline Parent-Gros, 17 June, 2020. Domaine AF Gros 16 Rue Pierre Joigneaux 21200 Beaune Tel: +33 3 80 24 61 26 www.af-gros.com More reports for AF Gros. …

From the 2022 vintage, A.-F. Gros will cede its Vosne Romanée Clos de la Fontaine to Domaine Gros Frere et Soeur, plus 10 ares of Richebourg. In exchange, they will acquire Clos Vougeot Musigni (14 ouvrées) and 13 ouvrées of Echezeaux les Loachausses. The Musigni parcel is equivalent to 0.60 ha – a substantial plot of what is often mentioned as one of the best parts of the Clos de Vougeot.

Winehog - with a passion – 12 Jun 19

Visit Domaine A.-F. Gros, tasting of the 2018s from cask

This was my first visit to Domaine A.-F. Gros after tasting the wines several times at Vosne Millisime, Trilogie and Grands Jours de Bourgogne. I have not

Est. reading time: 11 minutes

This seems to indicate some “horse trading” between A-F Gros and Bernard (Gros Frere et Soeur) but I haven’t seen that Michel or Anne will receive anything yet.
